metadata.career_relevance: Every professional Java codebase is full of generic classes — the entire Collections Framework (`ArrayList<T>`, `HashMap<K,V>`), Optional<T>, Comparator<T>, all functional interfaces. After this topic, you stop being a CONSUMER of generics and become a PRODUCER — you can build type-safe container classes, utility types, and data structures that work with any type. This unlocks a whole level of code reuse and API design that every senior Java developer uses constantly.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll define generic classes with one or more type parameters, understand the naming conventions, use type parameters in fields, constructors, and methods, and build reusable containers that work with any type — the same way Java's own Collections Framework is built.

concept_explanation.naming_conventions:
**Type parameter naming conventions:**

By convention, single uppercase letters are used:

| Letter | Meaning | Common usage |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `T` | Type | General-purpose type parameter |
| `E` | Element | Collection elements (ArrayList<E>) |
| `K` | Key | Map keys (Map<K,V>) |
| `V` | Value | Map values (Map<K,V>) |
| `R` | Return | Method return types |
| `N` | Number | Numeric types |
| `A`, `B` | Types | Multiple generic params |

```java
class Box<T> { ... }                 // simple container
class Pair<A, B> { ... }             // two different types
class Triple<A, B, C> { ... }        // three types
class NumberBox<N extends Number> {} // bounded (Topic 9.3)
```

These are conventions only — `T1`, `T2`, `TElement` are all valid. Follow the convention in professional code.
concept_explanation.type_erasure:
**Type erasure — what actually happens at runtime:**

Generic types are a COMPILE-TIME feature. The JVM (Java runtime) doesn't know about type parameters — they're erased at compilation:

```java
List<String> strings = new ArrayList<>();
List<Integer> ints   = new ArrayList<>();

// At runtime, both are just ArrayList (no type info)
System.out.println(strings.getClass() == ints.getClass());  // true!
```

The compiler:
1. Checks type safety (at compile time)
2. Inserts casts where needed (at compile time)
3. Removes the type parameters — replaces T with Object (or bound if bounded)

**Practical implications:**
- `new T()` doesn't compile — type not known at runtime
- `instanceof List<String>` doesn't compile — can only do `instanceof List`
- You CAN'T do `T[] array = new T[10]` — use `List<T>` instead
- At runtime, `List<String>` and `List<Integer>` are the same class

You don't need to understand type erasure deeply for daily work, but knowing it explains why certain things don't compile.

visual_aid.description: Show the generic class 'recipe' with type parameter: Box<T> class definition with T highlighted everywhere it appears (field type, constructor param, method return type). Below: three instantiations side by side — Box<String>, Box<Integer>, Box<Product> — each showing how T is substituted. Center: before/after comparison — Object-based container (with casts, ClassCastException arrow) vs generic container (no casts, compile error arrow for wrong type). Bottom: Pair<A,B> showing two type parameters, swap() returning Pair<B,A>.
visual_aid.alt_text: Generic class anatomy with type parameter substitution and before/after type safety comparison

worked_example.scenario: Build a generic `Result<T>` class — a common pattern in modern Java APIs (similar to Rust's Result, Kotlin's Result, and Java's own Optional). A Result is either SUCCESS (carrying a value of type T) or FAILURE (carrying an error message). This is used for operations that might fail without using exceptions.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Create `Result<T>` that can represent either a successful value or a failure:
- `Result.success(T value)` — factory method for success
- `Result.failure(String error)` — factory method for failure
- `isSuccess()`, `getValue()`, `getError()`
- `map(Function<T, R>)` — transform the value if successful
- `orElse(T default)` — get value or default on failure

Test with: division (might fail on zero), parsing (might fail on non-number).
worked_example.expected_output:
Success(5)
Failure(Division by zero)
Success(42)
Failure(Not a number: abc)
Value: 42
Invalid
things_to_notice: Result<T> is a generic class with one type parameter T — the type of the success value. The error message is always a String, so it doesn't need a type parameter.
The private constructor is called only through factory methods `success()` and `failure()`. This ensures the Result is always in a valid state (either value or error, never both).
The `map` method itself has its OWN type parameter `<R>` — this is a generic method on a generic class (Topic 9.2). The class is parameterized on T; the map method adds R for the output type.
Result.failure() returns `Result<R>` even though there's no R value — the failure case has no value, so the type parameter doesn't matter. The compiler needs the type to match.
Compare this to Optional<T>: Optional is essentially a specialized Result where failure has no message. Result is more informative — callers know WHY it failed.
map propagates failure: `parseIntSafe('abc').map(n -> 'Value: '+n)` — since parseIntSafe returned failure, map is skipped and the Failure propagates. orElse then returns 'Invalid'.

industry_examples.use_case: ArrayList<E>, HashMap<K,V>, Optional<T>, Stream<T>, Comparator<T> — these are all generic classes written exactly like what you learn today. The T in ArrayList<T>.add(T element) is the same T you declare in class ArrayList<E>. After this topic, the source code of these classes is readable.

Spring's ResponseEntity<T> is a generic class: `ResponseEntity<User>`, `ResponseEntity<List<Product>>`. Spring's `CrudRepository<T, ID>` is a generic interface where T is the entity type and ID is the primary key type. Generic design makes one Repository interface work for all entity types.

Every production REST API wraps responses: `class ApiResponse<T> { boolean success; T data; String message; }`. Returns `ApiResponse<User>` for user endpoints, `ApiResponse<List<Order>>` for order lists. One generic class serves all endpoints. This pattern is in every Flipkart, Razorpay, Zerodha internal API.

applications.interview_relevance:
Generic class questions test depth:

- **'What are generics in Java?'** — Parameterized types. Enforce type safety at compile time. Eliminate casts. Move ClassCastException from runtime to compile time.
- **'What is type erasure?'** — Generic type parameters are removed at compile time; replaced with Object (or bound). At runtime, List<String> and List<Integer> are the same class.
- **'What is a raw type?'** — Using a generic class without a type parameter (e.g., `List` instead of `List<String>`). Loses type safety. Never use in new code.
- **'Can you instantiate a type parameter?'** → No (`new T()` doesn't compile — type not known at runtime). Use factory methods or Class<T> (advanced).
applications.real_world_workflow:
Generic classes you'll write in production:

- `ApiResponse<T>` wrapper for REST controllers
- `Pair<A, B>` or `Tuple<A, B, C>` for multi-value returns
- `Result<T>` for operations that might fail (alternative to exceptions)
- `Cache<K, V>` wrapping a Map with TTL logic
- `Page<T>` for paginated results (Spring Data's Page<T> is exactly this)

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using raw types (`ArrayList list = new ArrayList()`) instead of parameterized types (`ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>()`).
common_gaps.remediation: Always provide the type parameter. Raw types lose all type safety. If you see IDE warnings about raw types, fix them.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: IDE warning: 'Raw use of parameterized class'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying to create instances of the type parameter: `new T()` — compile error.
common_gaps.remediation: Type parameters can't be instantiated at runtime (type erasure). Design around this: use factory methods, pass a Supplier<T>, or use reflection with Class<T> (advanced).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: 'Cannot instantiate the type T'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Writing `new Box<String>(value)` instead of `new Box<>(value)` — redundant type specification.
common_gaps.remediation: Since Java 7, use the diamond operator `<>` on the right side — the compiler infers the type from the left side declaration. `Box<String> b = new Box<>(value)` — no need to repeat String.

key_takeaways: Generics: parameterized types that move type errors from runtime to compile time
Generic class syntax: `class Name<T> { ... }` — T is a placeholder for any type
Type parameter T can be used for fields, parameters, return types inside the class
Diamond operator `<>`: `new Box<>()` — compiler infers the type
Multiple type parameters: `class Pair<A, B>` — common for key-value or result types
Type erasure: T is replaced with Object at runtime — explains why `new T()` doesn't work
Never use raw types — always provide the type parameter
Naming conventions: T (type), E (element), K/V (key/value), R (return)
Generic classes enable ONE class to serve MANY types safely — the foundation of the Collections Framework
Result<T> pattern: generic class for success/failure without exceptions

self_check_questions.question: Why does `new T()` fail to compile inside a generic class?
self_check_questions.answer: Type erasure. At runtime, T is just Object — the JVM doesn't know what actual type T represents. Since Java can't call `new Object()` and get a T-typed object back safely, the compiler prevents this. Solutions: accept a `Supplier<T>` parameter, accept a `Class<T>` and use reflection, or use a factory method.

self_check_questions.question: What's the difference between `Box<Object>` and a raw `Box`?
self_check_questions.answer: `Box<Object>` is a parameterized type where T = Object — it accepts any Object, but with full generic type checking. You'd need `Box<Object>` explicitly and can only get Object back. A raw `Box` (without any type parameter) is a pre-generics raw type — it accepts anything AND loses all type safety. They seem similar but raw type skips all generic checks (generates warnings), while `Box<Object>` is explicit and safe. Raw types are for backwards compatibility only — never use in new code.

self_check_questions.question: Write a generic `Box<T>` class with get(), set(), and isEmpty().

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Generic Methods** — defining a type parameter on a method itself, independent of the class. `<T extends Comparable<T>> T max(List<T>)` is a generic method — it works for any Comparable type without making the whole class generic. Generic methods are what make utility classes like `Collections.sort()` and `Collections.min()` work for any type.
